original_abstract: A growing body of research debates whether Americans who express a mix of liberal and conservative policy views are genuinely cross-pressured or essentially centrist. In a high-profile study, Fowler et al. (2023) introduce a mixture model to argue that most of these voters are "Downsian" ideologues whose views are well-summarized by a single dimension, implying that those with moderate estimated scores hold "genuinely centrist" positions. This article demonstrates that the method employed by Fowler et al. systematically overstates the prevalence of ideological consistency. Because the model’s "Conversian" category is defined as a single, homogenous cluster, it fails to accommodate the diversity of non-ideological belief systems described in classical public opinion literature. Using simulations and re-analysis of the original data, we show that the model frequently misclassifies truly cross-pressured and non-ideological voters as moderate ideologues simply because a line is more geometrically flexible than a point. Consequently, the conclusion that supposedly moderate voters support centrist policies remains unsupported. We suggest that distinguishing between stable idiosyncratic views and true centrism requires richer ordinal and panel data rather than binary cross-sectional surveys.
humanized_abstract: The literature has been debating the existence of truly cross-pressured citizens who hold a mix of liberal and conservative policy views. Some recent work suggests that such citizens are actually centrist, such as the work by Fowler et al. ( 2023) who use a mixture model to argue that nearly all mixed agenda voters are "Downsian" ideologues whose preferences are well-explained by a single-dimensional model and that those with moderate estimated scores are centrist. We show that the analysis in Fowler et al. exaggerates the homogeneity of the Conversational category. First, we observe that the modelling of this category as a single homogeneous cluster is not justifiable given the rich description of non-ideological belief systems that are depicted in the classical public opinion literature. Second, using Monte Carlo simulations of the mixture model and reanalysing the data in Fowler et al., we document cases in which the model assigns large numbers of truly cross-pressured and non-ideological individuals to the moderate ideologue category, not because of any difference in their policy preferences but because a line is a more flexible geometric form than a point. Hence, the claim that moderate individuals have centrist preferences remains unproven. We conclude by arguing that it will be necessary to have more refined types of data—that is, richer ordinal and panel data—that allow for more nuanced characterizations of citizens' preferences and thereby allow for discrimination between idiosyncratic and centrist preferences.
